food_subgroup: Cold tea beverages / Infused tea drinks

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term specifically for iced infused tea; closest parent 'tea beverage' (FOODON_03301099) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code AN0AN (Tea and tea-based beverages) used as closest parent; no specific code for iced infused tea.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 16313001 (Tea substance) used as closest parent; no specific concept for iced infused tea.

main_ingredients: tea leaves or herbal/fruit infusion blend

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 ml (300 g) of iced infused tea served in a tall glass over ice, as typically offered at a café.

nutrient_source.title: Tea, brewed, prepared with tap water (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB No. 14355)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Iced infused tea is nutritionally equivalent to brewed tea served cold. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for brewed tea (NDB 14355 / FDC ID 171917). Infused teas without added sugar or milk are nutritionally near-identical to plain brewed tea per 100 g. If the restaurant version contains added sugar or fruit juice, values would differ; this record represents the unsweetened baseline.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Beverages — Plain Tea and Near-Zero Carbohydrate Beverages Convention
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain brewed tea and unsweetened infused teas contain negligible carbohydrates (approximately 0.3 g per 100 g) and have no measurable glycemic response. The University of Sydney GI Database classifies plain tea as having an effectively zero GI. Per Veritas convention for zero-carbohydrate beverages, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel value) and gi_category is set to 'low'. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (1 × 0.9 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.009, rounded to 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (3 mg per 100 g); no added salt
hypertension.guidance: Unsweetened iced infused tea is an excellent low-sodium beverage choice for individuals managing hypertension, contributing negligible sodium and providing hydration without cardiovascular burden.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content and effectively zero glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Unsweetened iced infused tea has a negligible glycemic impact and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, patients should confirm no added sugar or sweetened syrups are included in the restaurant preparation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and polyphenol content from tea infusion
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Unsweetened iced infused tea contains no saturated fat or cholesterol and may provide cardioprotective polyphenols (catechins, flavonoids) depending on the infusion blend, making it a heart-friendly beverage choice.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Iced Infused Tea is a chilled beverage made by steeping tea leaves, herbs, fruits, or botanical blends in cold or hot water and then serving the resulting infusion over ice. The preparation method varies: cold infusion involves steeping ingredients directly in cold water for several hours, while hot infusion involves brewing at elevated temperature and then chilling. The flavour profile depends on the infusion blend — common varieties include floral (hibiscus, rose), fruity (berry, citrus), herbal (mint, chamomile), or classic tea (green, black, white). The texture is light and refreshing with no viscosity. At Mingle - Funan in Singapore, this beverage is offered as a café-style drink priced at SGD 6.00. Nutritionally, unsweetened iced infused tea is essentially calorie-free (approximately 1 kcal per 100 g), contains negligible protein and fat, and has a trace carbohydrate content of approximately 0.3 g per 100 g. It has a very low sodium content (3 mg per 100 g) and provides small amounts of potassium and magnesium. The glycemic index is effectively zero, making it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. Tea polyphenols such as catechins and flavonoids may confer antioxidant and cardioprotective benefits. Clinically, the primary caution is the potential for added sugar or sweetened syrups in restaurant preparations, which would significantly alter the glycemic and caloric profile. Patients with diabetes should confirm the preparation is unsweetened.
common_user_queries: Is iced infused tea good for diabetics?
How many calories in iced infused tea?
Can I drink iced tea if I have high blood pressure?
Is iced infused tea gluten free?

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry exists for 'Iced Infused Tea' as a named dish in any approved primary source. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy brewed tea data, which is the closest defensible approved-source match. The restaurant preparation at Mingle - Funan may include added ingredients (syrups, fruit juice, sweeteners) that would alter the nutritional profile. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are based on plain unsweetened brewed tea (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FDC ID 171917) as the closest approved-source analogue. If the restaurant preparation includes added sugar, fruit juice, or syrups, energy_kcal and carbohydrate_g would be substantially higher. 2. protein_g is naturally zero as plain tea contains no protein by nature. fat_g is naturally zero as plain tea contains no fat by nature. Confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 3. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per Veritas convention for near-zero-carbohydrate beverages. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 given negligible carbohydrate content. 4. The cuisine tag 'Italian' in the input record reflects the restaurant's cuisine category (Mingle - Funan is an Italian-style café) rather than the origin of iced infused tea itself, which is a globally generic beverage. Cuisine has been corrected to 'International / Café'. 5. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the food is served in Singapore. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are 0.0 as confirmed by USDA source for plain brewed tea. 7. All vitamin and mineral fields not reported as zero are set based on USDA SR Legacy data for brewed tea. selenium_mcg is 0.0 per USDA data. 8. Ontology codes use closest_parent match type as no exact ontology term exists for iced infused tea specifically.

Set glycemic_index to 1 (sentinel) and gi_category to 'low' per Veritas zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule.
Provided multilingual names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean serving context rules.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency — all entries match verbatim.
Documented all ontology codes as closest_parent with gap notes.
